section.title: Jeremiah Buys a Field
section.sectionRationale: With the Babylonian army camped outside the walls and Jeremiah himself imprisoned for prophesying the city's fall, God commands the strangest possible act of hope: buy a field. The purchase, carefully witnessed and sealed, becomes a living sign that even as Jerusalem falls, God's covenant faithfulness guarantees a future when houses, fields, and vineyards will again be bought in this land.
section.thesis: Jeremiah's purchase of a field he may never see again is an act of hope grounded not in the immediate circumstances, which are genuinely catastrophic, but in the character of the God for whom nothing is too difficult, whose everlasting covenant guarantees restoration on the other side of deserved judgment.
section.frameNote: Five scenes: Jeremiah imprisoned and confronted by Zedekiah for his prophecy of doom; God's command to buy the field and Jeremiah's careful, witnessed purchase; Jeremiah's own prayer wrestling with the apparent contradiction between the command and the coming disaster; God's reply naming the real reasons for Jerusalem's judgment; and God's climactic promise of an everlasting covenant and fields bought again in the land.

units.label: Jeremiah imprisoned, Zedekiah's accusation
units.note: why do you prophesy like this?

verses.text: This is the word that came to Jeremiah from the LORD in the tenth year of Zedekiah king of Judah, which was the eighteenth year of Nebuchadnezzar.
At that time the army of the king of Babylon was besieging Jerusalem, and Jeremiah the prophet was imprisoned in the courtyard of the guard, which was in the palace of the king of Judah.

For Zedekiah king of Judah had imprisoned him, saying: “Why are you prophesying like this? You claim that the LORD says, ‘Behold, I am about to deliver this city into the hand of the king of Babylon, and he will capture it.

Zedekiah king of Judah will not escape from the hands of the Chaldeans, but he will surely be delivered into the hand of the king of Babylon, and will speak with him face to face and see him eye to eye.
He will take Zedekiah to Babylon, where he will stay until I attend to him, declares the LORD. If you fight against the Chaldeans, you will not succeed.’”
Jeremiah replied, “The word of the LORD came to me, saying:
Behold! Hanamel, the son of your uncle Shallum, is coming to you to say, ‘Buy for yourself my field in Anathoth, for you have the right of {{geullah|redemption}} to buy it.’

Then, as the LORD had said, my cousin Hanamel came to me in the courtyard of the guard and urged me, ‘Please buy my field in Anathoth in the land of Benjamin, for you own the right of inheritance and redemption. Buy it for yourself.’” 
Then I knew that this was the word of the LORD.
So I bought the field in Anathoth from my cousin Hanamel, and I weighed out seventeen shekels of silver.
I signed and sealed the deed, called in witnesses, and weighed out the silver on the scales.
Then I took the deed of purchase, the sealed copy with its terms and conditions, as well as the open copy,

and I gave this deed to Baruch son of Neriah, the son of Mahseiah, in the sight of my cousin Hanamel and the witnesses who were signing the purchase agreement and all the Jews sitting in the courtyard of the guard.
In their sight I instructed Baruch,

“This is what the LORD of Hosts, the God of Israel, says: Take these deeds, both the sealed copy and the open copy of the deed of purchase, and put them in a clay jar to preserve them for a long time.
For this is what the LORD of Hosts, the God of Israel, says: Houses, fields, and vineyards will again be bought in this land.”

units.label: Jeremiah's prayer: nothing too difficult for You

verses.text: After I had given the deed of purchase to Baruch son of Neriah, I prayed to the LORD:
“Oh, Lord GOD! You have made the heavens and the earth by Your great power and outstretched arm. Nothing is too {{pala|difficult}} for You!
You show loving devotion to thousands but lay the iniquity of the fathers into the laps of their children after them, O great and mighty God whose name is the LORD of Hosts,
the One great in counsel and mighty in deed, whose eyes are on all the ways of the sons of men, to reward each one according to his ways and according to the fruit of his deeds.
You performed signs and wonders in the land of Egypt, and You do so to this very day, both in Israel and among all mankind. And You have made a name for Yourself, as is the case to this day.
You brought Your people Israel out of the land of Egypt with signs and wonders, with a strong hand and an outstretched arm, and with great terror.
You gave them this land that You had sworn to give their fathers, a land flowing with milk and honey.
They came in and possessed it, but they did not obey Your voice or walk in Your law. They failed to perform all that You commanded them to do, and so You have brought upon them all this disaster.

See how the siege ramps are mounted against the city to capture it. And by sword and famine and plague, the city has been given into the hands of the Chaldeans who are fighting against it. What You have spoken has happened, as You now see!
Yet You, O Lord GOD, have said to me, ‘Buy for yourself the field with silver and call in witnesses, even though the city has been delivered into the hands of the Chaldeans!’”

units.label: God's reply: the real reasons for judgment

verses.text: Then the word of the LORD came to Jeremiah:
“Behold, I am the LORD, the God of all flesh. Is anything too difficult for Me?
Therefore this is what the LORD says: Behold, I am about to deliver this city into the hands of the Chaldeans and of Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon, who will capture it.

And the Chaldeans who are fighting against this city will come in, set it on fire, and burn it, along with the houses of those who provoked Me to anger by burning incense to Baal on their rooftops and by pouring out drink offerings to other gods.
For the children of Israel and of Judah have done nothing but evil in My sight from their youth; indeed, they have done nothing but provoke Me to anger by the work of their hands, declares the LORD.
For this city has aroused My wrath and fury from the day it was built until now. Therefore I will remove it from My presence

because of all the evil the children of Israel and of Judah have done to provoke Me to anger, they, their kings, their officials, their priests and prophets, the men of Judah, and the residents of Jerusalem.
They have turned their backs to Me and not their faces. Though I taught them again and again, they would not listen or respond to discipline.
They have placed their abominations in the house that bears My Name, and so have defiled it.

They have built the high places of Baal in the Valley of Hinnom to make their sons and daughters pass through the fire to Molech, something I never commanded them, nor had it ever entered My mind, that they should commit such an abomination and cause Judah to sin.

units.label: An everlasting covenant, fields bought again

verses.text: Now therefore, about this city of which you say, ‘It will be delivered into the hand of the king of Babylon by sword and famine and plague,’ this is what the LORD, the God of Israel, says:
I will surely gather My people from all the lands to which I have banished them in My furious anger and great wrath, and I will return them to this place and make them dwell in safety.
They will be My people, and I will be their God.
I will give them one heart and one way, so that they will always fear Me for their own good and for the good of their children after them.
I will make an {{beritOlam|everlasting covenant}} with them: I will never turn away from doing good to them, and I will put My fear in their hearts, so that they will never turn away from Me.
Yes, I will rejoice in doing them good, and I will faithfully plant them in this land with all My heart and with all My soul.
For this is what the LORD says: Just as I have brought all this great disaster on this people, so I will bring on them all the good I have promised them.
And fields will be bought in this land about which you are saying, ‘It is a desolation, without man or beast; it has been delivered into the hands of the Chaldeans.’

Fields will be purchased with silver, and deeds will be signed, sealed, and witnessed in the land of Benjamin, in the areas surrounding Jerusalem, and in the cities of Judah, the cities of the hill country, the foothills, and the Negev, because I will restore them from captivity, declares the LORD.”

geullah.gloss: The right of redemption, the obligation of a near kinsman to buy back family land so it stays within the family, Leviticus 25:25. Jeremiah's purchase of Hanamel's field is an act of kinsman redemption at the very moment redemption itself seemed impossible.

pala.gloss: To be too difficult, too wonderful, beyond ordinary power. Jeremiah asks whether anything is too pala for the God who made heaven and earth; God answers his own question in verse 27, turning the prophet's uncertain praise into a settled promise.

beritOlam.gloss: An everlasting covenant, one God binds Himself to keep forever. Here it guarantees that God will never turn away from doing good to His people and will put His own fear in their hearts so they never turn away from Him, an unbreakable promise on the far side of a fully deserved judgment.

basic.context: With Babylon's army besieging Jerusalem and Jeremiah himself imprisoned for prophesying its fall, God commands him to buy a field from his cousin Hanamel, an act that made no earthly sense given the coming disaster. Jeremiah obeys, has the deed carefully sealed and witnessed, then honestly prays through his confusion, only to hear God explain both the real reasons for judgment and His unshakable promise of future restoration.
basic.hermeneutics: The field purchase is a prophetic sign act, a real estate transaction turned into a message: even as the city falls, God's covenant with His people has a future. Jeremiah's prayer shows that obedience and honest wrestling are not opposites; he obeys the strange command first and then brings his real confusion to God afterward.
basic.originalLanguages: Jeremiah buys the field under the ancient right of geullah, kinsman redemption, keeping family land in the family even as all land seems about to be lost. His prayer asks whether anything is too pala, too difficult, for God, and God's own answer grounds the coming beritOlam, everlasting covenant, in that same limitless power.
basic.typology: The careful, sealed, and witnessed deed preserved in a clay jar for a long time anticipates a redemption secured and guaranteed in writing, pointing forward to the certainty of the inheritance kept in Heaven for believers, 1 Peter 1:3-4. The everlasting covenant of one heart and one way anticipates the internal transformation Christ accomplishes by His Spirit.
basic.christ: Jeremiah's purchase pictures a redeemer paying a price for land under judgment because of a coming restoration he will not personally see fulfilled, a shadow of Christ, who purchased His people at the highest price while judgment was still falling, securing an inheritance He guarantees though its fullness is still to come, Ephesians 1:13-14.
basic.god: God is both perfectly just, naming with unflinching honesty the specific idolatries that have provoked His wrath, and perfectly faithful, for whom nothing is too difficult, including binding Himself to an everlasting covenant of good toward a people who have deserved only judgment.
basic.crossReferences: Leviticus 25:25 establishes the kinsman's right of redemption Jeremiah exercises. Ephesians 1:13-14 describes the Holy Spirit as the guarantee of an inheritance secured but not yet fully possessed. Luke 1:37, echoing Genesis 18:14, affirms that nothing is impossible with God. Ezekiel 36:26-27 parallels the promise of one heart and God's Spirit within His people.
basic.oneStory: In the darkest moment of the book, with the city already as good as fallen, God commands an act of costly, concrete hope: buy the field, because houses and fields will be bought again. That pattern, redemption purchased in the very moment judgment falls, reaches its fullest form in Christ, who purchased an everlasting inheritance for His people at the cross, guaranteed by His Spirit until its full possession.

context.literary: A narrative sign act framed by Jeremiah's own extended prayer and God's extended reply, giving readers rare access to the prophet's internal wrestling as well as to God's own reasoning, both for judgment and for future mercy.
context.historical: Set in the tenth year of Zedekiah, the eighteenth of Nebuchadnezzar, during the final siege of Jerusalem, when the city's fall was no longer a distant prophecy but a present, visible reality, siege ramps already mounted against its walls.
context.canonical: This chapter supplies one of Scripture's most vivid pictures of hope enacted in the middle of judgment, a purchased field standing as physical, legal evidence that God's promises of restoration are as real and binding as any human contract.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To demonstrate through a concrete, legally documented act that God's promise of future restoration is real and certain even as His judgment on Jerusalem's sin is fully carried out, and to model honest prayer that obeys first and questions afterward.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This is a specific historical sign act tied to a specific field and a specific crisis; it does not prescribe that believers must buy property as a sign of faith in every hardship. It does model the enduring pattern of costly, concrete hope grounded in God's character rather than present circumstances.

debates.question: Why does Jeremiah pray as though wrestling with God's command after he has already obeyed it?

views.label: Honest processing after obedience, not doubt before it
views.case: Jeremiah buys the field first, in verses 9 to 15, and only afterward, in verse 16, brings his honest confusion to God, modeling obedience that does not require full understanding first, followed by real wrestling in prayer.

views.label: A literary device to let God's reasoning be heard
views.case: The prayer's careful recitation of God's past power and Judah's specific sins creates the occasion for God's own extended reply in verses 26 to 44, giving the passage a structure that lets both the case for judgment and the promise of mercy be stated fully.

debates.bearing: Either way, the passage shows that obeying God's word does not require first resolving every question, and that God welcomes honest prayer even from those who have already obeyed Him.

study.originalLanguages: Jeremiah exercises the geullah, the kinsman's right of redemption, buying land that seems about to be worthless. His prayer turns on whether anything is too pala, too wonderful or difficult, for the God who made heaven and earth, and God's reply grounds the promised beritOlam, the everlasting covenant, in that very same unlimited power.

typology.type: The sealed deed of purchase preserved for a long time, Jeremiah 32:14-15
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates the Holy Spirit as the seal and guarantee of an inheritance kept secure for believers until they fully possess it, Ephesians 1:13-14.

typology.type: One heart and one way given by God, Jeremiah 32:39
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates the new heart and new spirit God gives His people through the Spirit, Ezekiel 36:26-27, fulfilled in the internal transformation Christ accomplishes.

study.christ: Jeremiah's purchase of a field under judgment, secured by a sealed deed for a future he might not personally see, pictures a redemption paid for in advance of its full realization, a pattern fulfilled in Christ, who purchased His people at the cross even as judgment for sin was being poured out, and who guarantees their inheritance by His Spirit until it is fully possessed, Ephesians 1:13-14. The one heart and one way God promises to give is the very transformation the Spirit of Christ accomplishes in those united to Him.
study.god: God names Judah's sin with total honesty, idolatry to Baal and even child sacrifice to Molech, showing His judgment is neither arbitrary nor disproportionate. Yet in the same breath He answers His own rhetorical question, nothing is too difficult for Him, by binding Himself to an everlasting covenant of good toward the very people whose sin He has just described, a promise as certain as His power to make heaven and earth.

crossReferences.note: Establishes the kinsman's right of redemption that Jeremiah exercises in buying Hanamel's field.
The Holy Spirit as the guarantee of an inheritance secured but not yet fully possessed parallels the sealed deed kept for a future restoration.
Echoes the same truth Jeremiah confesses, that nothing is impossible with God.
Parallels the promise of one heart and God's Spirit placed within His people, fulfilled through the Spirit of Christ.

study.oneStory: At the darkest moment of the siege, God commands a concrete, costly act of hope, buy the field, because houses and fields will be bought again in this land. That pattern of redemption enacted in the very moment judgment falls reaches its fullest form in Christ, who purchased an everlasting inheritance for His people at the cross, sealed by His Spirit until its full and certain possession.

quiz.q: Why does Jeremiah buy the field in Anathoth despite the coming Babylonian conquest?

options.text: God commands it as a prophetic sign that houses, fields, and vineyards will again be bought in the land after the exile.
options.note: Verse 15 explicitly states the purchase is a sign of future restoration, not a wise financial decision in itself.

options.text: He believed the siege would be lifted immediately.
options.note: Jeremiah has already prophesied the city's certain fall; the purchase is a sign of future hope, not immediate deliverance.

options.text: Hanamel offered him the field for free.
options.note: Jeremiah pays seventeen shekels of silver for the field, it was not free.

options.text: It was simply a routine, unremarkable transaction with no larger significance.
options.note: The elaborate sealing, witnessing, and preservation of the deed mark this as a deliberate, significant sign act.

quiz.q: What tension does Jeremiah bring to God in his prayer in verses 16 to 25?

options.text: How buying a field could make sense when the city is already being delivered into Babylon's hands.
options.note: Verse 25 makes the tension explicit, God commanded the purchase even as the city was already falling.

options.text: Whether God actually exists.
options.note: Jeremiah's prayer affirms God's power and past deeds throughout; it does not question His existence.

options.text: Whether Hanamel had cheated him in the sale.
options.note: The prayer does not concern the fairness of the transaction but the apparent contradiction between the command and the coming disaster.

options.text: Whether he had followed the correct legal procedure for the purchase.
options.note: The legal procedure is described as carefully and correctly followed; the tension is theological, not procedural.

quiz.q: What reasons does God give for Jerusalem's coming destruction in verses 26 to 35?

options.text: Persistent idolatry, including burning incense to Baal and child sacrifice to Molech, provoking God's anger for generations.
options.note: Verses 29 to 35 specifically name these sins as the cause of the coming judgment.

options.text: God gives no reasons and the destruction is arbitrary.
options.note: God gives extensive, specific reasons rooted in Judah's persistent idolatry and disobedience.

options.text: Purely political and military failures with no moral or spiritual cause.
options.note: The stated causes are explicitly moral and spiritual, idolatry and rebellion, not merely political failure.

options.text: The actions of a single recent king alone.
options.note: The text describes sin committed by the people, their kings, officials, priests, and prophets across generations, not one king alone.

quiz.q: What does God promise in the everlasting covenant of verses 36 to 44?

options.text: He will gather His scattered people, give them one heart and one way, and never turn away from doing good to them, so fields will be bought again in the land.
options.note: Verses 37 to 44 detail this full promise of gathering, inward transformation, and restored ordinary life.

options.text: That Jerusalem will never fall to Babylon at all.
options.note: The chapter has already affirmed the city's certain fall; the everlasting covenant concerns restoration after that judgment.

options.text: That the covenant depends entirely on the people's future good behavior.
options.note: God explicitly promises He will put His fear in their hearts so they will not turn away, grounding the covenant in His own action, not merely their performance.

options.text: That only a distant future generation, with no connection to those in exile, will benefit.
options.note: The promise is to gather His people from where they have been banished, directly addressing the very generation facing exile.

quiz.q: What does the careful preservation of the sealed deed in a clay jar, verses 14 to 15, anticipate?

options.text: The certainty of a future inheritance kept secure for God's people, paralleling the Spirit as the guarantee of the believer's inheritance in Christ.
options.note: Ephesians 1:13-14 describes the Spirit as a seal and guarantee, echoing the sealed deed kept for a future, certain fulfillment.

options.text: Nothing beyond a routine legal formality of the time.
options.note: The elaborate preservation for a long time signals deliberate symbolic significance, not mere routine formality.

options.text: That the promise would never actually be fulfilled.
options.note: The whole point of preserving the deed is to testify that fields would indeed be bought again, a promise of certain fulfillment.

options.text: A warning that the field itself was worthless.
options.note: The deed's preservation testifies to the field's future value and the certainty of restoration, not worthlessness.

questions: Jeremiah bought a field he might never personally use, trusting a promise beyond his own lifetime. What costly, concrete step of hope might God be asking of you right now?
Jeremiah obeyed first and brought his honest confusion to God afterward. Where do you need to obey God before you fully understand, trusting Him with your questions along the way?
God named Judah's specific sins honestly rather than vaguely. Is there a specific sin you have been avoiding naming honestly before God?
God asked, is anything too difficult for Me, and then answered His own question with a promise of restoration. Where in your life do you need to remember that nothing is too difficult for God?
God promised to give His people one heart and one way. Where is there a divided heart in you that needs God's unifying work?

translationNotes.note: Seventeen shekels of silver was a modest price, suggesting the field's market value had already collapsed under the siege, making the purchase even more clearly an act of faith rather than sound economic investment.

Jeremiah's prayer places God's own command, buy the field, in direct tension with the visible, undeniable fact of the city's fall, a tension he brings honestly to God rather than resolving on his own before praying.

It never entered My mind underscores that child sacrifice to Molech was never God's intention or command, a practice utterly foreign to His character, directly contradicting any later claim that such rites had divine sanction.
